title: Making a NAND Backup
requirements: An SD card or USB drive with at least 512MB free (1GB+ recommended), FAT32 formatted
BootMii installed (for Method 1) — otherwise use Method 2 below
warnings: This backup is !!your safety net!! for any future problems — don't skip it, and don't lose it once you have it.
_note: A NAND backup is a full copy of your Wii's internal system memory. Keep one before doing any further modding — it's what lets you recover from a bad update, a broken IOS, or most other software-level bricks. This is the same process str2hax walks you through inline, generalized for consoles that already have BootMii or need the nanddumper@IOS alternative instead.

steps.title: Before you start
steps.body:
There are two ways to make a NAND backup:

- **Method 1: BootMii** — the standard way, if BootMii is already installed on your console.
- **Method 2: nanddumper@IOS** — a Homebrew Channel app that works even without BootMii, and is required on consoles like the **Wii mini** that don't have a working SD card slot for BootMii to use directly (use a USB drive with it instead).

First, let's check your SD card or USB drive is FAT32.

steps.title: Method 1: Launch BootMii
steps.body:
Open the **Homebrew Channel**. Press the **HOME** button on your Wii Remote, then select **Launch BootMii**.

(If the button doesn't appear, BootMii isn't installed — use Method 2 below, or see the Relaunching the HackMii Installer guide to install it.)

You should see BootMii's main menu. Use the **GameCube controller's D-pad** or the **console's POWER/RESET buttons** (POWER = move right, RESET = select).

steps.title: Method 1: Make the backup
steps.body:
In BootMii, select the **Options** button (the gears icon). Then select the first icon on the left (NAND Backup).

BootMii dumps your NAND and shows the progress. "Bad Blocks" are normal — they come from the factory. When the dump finishes, BootMii verifies the backup. Ideally all blocks are green.

Press the **Back** button (arrow icon), then select either the **Wii Menu** or **Homebrew Channel** to exit BootMii.

Power off the Wii, then skip ahead to **Save the backup** below.

troubleshoot.label: The backup failed or has errors

steps.title: Method 2: Put nanddumper@IOS on your SD card
steps.body: Only do this if you're not using BootMii. Graphite downloads **nanddumper@IOS** and unpacks it into the `apps` folder on your SD card or USB drive.

steps.title: Method 2: Run nanddumper@IOS
steps.body:
Insert the SD card or USB drive into your Wii and open the **Homebrew Channel**.

1. Press **1** to confirm your storage device, then open **nanddumper@IOS**.
2. Press any button (except HOME, START, or EJECT) to start the dump.
3. Wait for it to finish, then press any button to exit. The files save to `/wii/backups/` on your storage device.

On your computer afterward, rename `DATE_SERIAL_nand_XX.bin` to `nand.bin` and `SERIAL_keys.bin` to `keys.bin` before using them with any restore tool that expects those exact names.

troubleshoot.label: nanddumper@IOS won't run or errors out

steps.title: Save your NAND backup
steps.body:
Take the SD card out of the Wii and plug it back into this computer.

Press the button and Graphite grabs the backup the console just made (`nand.bin` and `keys.bin`), copies it into a folder on your **Desktop**, and clears the files off the SD card to free up space. If a backup folder already exists, it saves a new one alongside it.

If you used Method 2 (nanddumper@IOS), rename the files as described above **before** running this step, so Graphite recognizes them.

steps.title: You're done
steps.body: Keep this backup somewhere safe — ideally more than one place. If your Wii ever gets stuck or bricked, the factory reset / NAND restore guide covers flashing this backup back onto the console.
